name: Thule Society
description: The Thule Society (Thule-Gesellschaft) was a German occultist and Völkisch group founded in Munich shortly after World War I. It is primarily known as the organization that sponsored the German Workers' Party (DAP), later renamed the Nazi Party. The Society's name derives from Ultima Thule, a mythical northern land mentioned in ancient Greek and Roman literature. Proponents claim the Thule Society sought to research and revive an ancient Aryan race, believing that a superior Nordic race originated in Thule and possessed secret esoteric knowledge and psychic powers. They are often associated with the development of Nazi ideology and the occult practices attributed to the Nazi regime. Key beliefs included Aryan racial supremacy, anti-Semitism, and the search for lost esoteric wisdom. Evidence cited for these claims often involves the Society's membership (which included prominent Nazi figures), its use of esoteric symbols like the swastika, and the writings of its members, which promoted racist and occult ideas. Mainstream historical perspectives generally portray the Thule Society as a relatively small and marginal group whose influence on the Nazi Party has been exaggerated. Historians emphasize that while some early Nazi members were associated with the Society, its occult beliefs were not central to Nazi ideology, which was primarily driven by political and economic factors, as well as racial nationalism. The alleged connection between the Thule Society and advanced technology or paranormal phenomena is widely considered to be pseudoscientific and lacking credible evidence. The Society's focus on racial purity and anti-Semitism contributed to the broader climate of hatred and violence that characterized the Nazi era.
subtitle: German occultist and Völkisch group.

claims.claim: The Thule Society sought to revive an ancient Aryan race with superior powers.
claims.evidence: The Society's name, derived from Ultima Thule, and the writings of its members expressing belief in a Nordic master race and their hidden knowledge.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians argue that the concept of an 'Aryan race' is a pseudoscientific construct, and there's no credible evidence of psychic powers or ancient esoteric knowledge passed down through such a race.

claims.claim: The Thule Society directly influenced the development of Nazi ideology.
claims.evidence: The fact that several early Nazi leaders were members of the Thule Society, suggesting a direct link between the Society's beliefs and Nazi policies.
claims.criticism: Historians argue that while some Nazis were associated with the Thule Society, its occult beliefs were not central to Nazi ideology, which was primarily driven by political, economic, and racial nationalist factors. The Society was just one of many Völkisch groups at the time.

claims.claim: The Thule Society was involved in secret research into advanced technology or paranormal phenomena.
claims.evidence: Legends and conspiracy theories that link the Thule Society to alleged Nazi UFO programs and secret weapons research.
claims.criticism: There is no credible evidence to support these claims. They are generally considered to be part of a larger body of pseudoscientific and conspiratorial narratives surrounding Nazi Germany.

keyFigures.name: Karl Haushofer
keyFigures.role: Influential member, associated with geopolitics and occultism

keyFigures.name: Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke
keyFigures.role: Historian who critically examined the Thule Society and its influence

mythologyConnections: Nordic mythology (especially concepts of a lost Hyperborean civilization)
The concept of Ultima Thule as a mythical northern land

timeline.event: Thule Society sponsors the German Workers' Party (DAP)
The Thule Society gradually declines in influence as the Nazi Party gains power

sources.author: Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke

sources.title: Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke
